questions.text: Are exposed employees informed of the existence and location of permit spaces via posted danger signs or other equally effective means?

questions.text: If the employer has decided that employees will enter permit spaces, has a written permit space program been developed and implemented?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.146(k)(1): An employer who designates rescue and emergency services pursuant to paragraph (d)(9) of this section shall: (k)(1)(i) evaluate a prospective rescuer's ability to respond to a rescue summons in a timely manner, considering the hazard(s) identified Note to paragraph (k)(1)(i): What will be considered timely will vary according to the specific hazards involved in each entry. references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.335(a)(1)(i): Employees working in areas where there are potential electrical hazards shall be provided with, and shall use, electrical protective equipment that is appropriate for the specific parts of the body to be protected and for the work to be performed. (Note: OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart S is fundamentally a performance-based standard that defines the required level of electrical safety and protection outcomes, without prescribing a specific method for achieving them. questions.text: Do employees at all levels of the organization understand what ergonomics is and can identify ergonomic risk factors (awkward postures, forces, repetitive motion, etc.)?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: Because there is no specific OSHA standard for ergonomics, compliance is assessed under the General Duty Clause. To demonstrate compliance, an employer must show they have implemented feasible means of abatement; this includes training employees to recognize early signs of MSDs and identify risk factors such as repetition, force, and awkward postures before injuries occur.

questions.text: Do you have a formal process for quantifying risk across workstations and tasks?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: Subjective observation is often insufficient to establish a "recognized hazard" under the General Duty Clause. Employers should utilize validated ergonomic assessment tools (such as the NIOSH Lifting Equation, RULA, REBA, or Strain Index) to objectively quantify risk levels. This data provides the necessary evidence to justify capital expenditures for engineering controls.

questions.text: Do you have a quantitative measure for all physical demands within each job (e.g., push forces are 50 lbs, horizontal reach in front of the body is 18 inches?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: To determine if a specific task exceeds the physical capabilities of the workforce, and thus constitutes a recognized hazard, accurate data is required. Documenting specific force, frequency, and geometry measurements is essential for comparing job demands against known ergonomic thresholds (such as Snook Tables or Liberty Mutual Tables) and designing appropriate abatements..

questions.text: Do you have a formal process in place for procurement or design of equipment, workstations, hand tools, etc., to ensure ergonomic design specifications are being considered?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: While OSHA does not explicitly mandate pre-purchase ergonomic evaluations, "Prevention through Design" is considered the most effective and feasible means of abatement. Integrating ergonomic specifications into the procurement process eliminates hazards before they enter the facility, avoiding the higher costs and compliance risks associated with retrofitting equipment later.

questions.text: Do you have a formal ergonomics process with roles and responsibilities clearly defined at all levels of the organization?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: For an ergonomics program to be considered "effective" during an OSHA inspection, it must be more than a written policy; it must be operational. Clearly defining roles ensures that hazard identification, reporting, and abatement are not ad-hoc activities but are integrated into the daily management system, satisfying the employer's ongoing duty to provide a safe workplace.

questions.text: Can you prioritize corrective actions within your ergonomics program based on cost, ease of implementation, and overall risk?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: Section 5(a)(1) requires employers to use "feasible" means to abate hazards. By maintaining a prioritized list of corrective actions based on risk severity and implementation difficulty, an employer demonstrates a good-faith effort to systematically address hazards, even if immediate resolution of all issues is not financially or operationally possible simultaneously.

questions.text: Can you demonstrate the collection of ergonomic solutions that have been put in place that have a proven record of risk reduction or benefits to productivity or quality?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: Mere implementation of a change is not sufficient to satisfy an abatement requirement; the change must actually reduce the hazard. Documenting "after" assessments or case studies serves as proof that the engineering or administrative controls implemented were effective in removing or reducing the recognized hazard to an acceptable level.

questions.text: Have you identified the light-duty task within each job?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: implement engineering controls, such as mechanical lifting devices, adjustable workstations, or conveyor systems, reduce physical exertion and repetitive motion by making changes to task design and job rotation, provide employees with ergonomically designed tools and anti-fatigue matting, and train employees on how to prevent and recognize signs of Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs), how to apply proper body mechanics, and appropriate engineering controls, and personal protective equipment and work practices to reduce the risk of Ergonomic hazards.

Note: Identifying light-duty or restricted-work options proactively is a key component of Medical Management. While not a direct engineering control, this administrative strategy prevents the aggravation of existing MSDs (ensuring they do not progress to "serious physical harm") and supports the employer’s obligation to minimize the severity of workplace injuries.

questions.text: Are lithium batteries stored safely in accordance with fire safety and hazardous materials regulations?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970, which mandates that: 'Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees’ employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. While OSHA has not yet enacted a specific standard within 29 CFR 1910 governing the storage and charging of lithium-ion batteries in General Industry, the catastrophic failure of these units (thermal runaway) is a growing trend and a universally recognized hazard in the industrial sector; consequently, this hazard is enforceable under the General Duty Clause.

While OSHA has not enacted a specific standard exclusively governing lithium-ion batteries, the hazards associated with their storage, charging, and maintenance are regulated under the General Duty Clause (Section 5(a)(1) of the OSH Act) and a broad range of existing General Industry standards. To ensure compliance, the facility must align its practices with 1910 Subpart L (Fire Protection), 1910.39 (Fire Prevention Plans), and 1910.120 (Hazardous Waste Operation and Emergency Response) to address fire and emergency risks. Operational and handling hazards must be managed through 1910 Subpart S (Electrical), 1910.147 (The Control of Hazardous Energy - Lockout/Tagout), and 1910.178 (Powered Industrial Trucks). Furthermore, employee protection and chemical safety requirements are enforceable under 1910.119 (Process Safety Management), 1910.132 (Personal Protective Equipment), 1910.134 (Respiratory Protection), 1910.151 (Medical Services and First Aid), 1910.1000 (Air Contaminants), 1910.1020 (Access to Employee Exposure and Medical Records), and 1910.1200 (Hazard Communication).

questions.text: Are appropriate PPE and safety protocols followed when using higher potential risk tools, such as portable grinders or saws?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.132(a): Protective equipment, including personal protective equipment for eyes, face, head, and extremities, protective clothing, respiratory devices, and protective shields and barriers, shall be provided, used, and maintained in a sanitary and reliable condition wherever it is necessary by reason of hazards of processes or environment, chemical hazards, radiological hazards, or mechanical irritants encountered in a manner capable of causing injury or impairment in the function of any part of the body through absorption, inhalation or physical contact.

29 CFR 1910.132(d)(1): The employer shall assess the workplace to determine if hazards are present, or are likely to be present, which necessitate the use of personal protective equipment. If such hazards are present, or likely to be present, the employer shall: (i) Select, and have each affected employee use, the types of personal protective equipment that will protect the affected employee from the hazards identified in the hazard assessment; (ii) Communicate selection decisions to each affected employee; and (iii) Select personal protective equipment that properly fits each affected employee.

questions.text: Do you currently store chemicals in an approved container, cabinet, or room?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.106(a)(35): Approved unless otherwise indicated, approved, or listed by a nationally recognized testing laboratory. Refer to § 1910.7 for the definition of nationally recognized testing laboratory.

29 CFR 1910.106(d)(2)(i): General. Only approved containers and portable tanks shall be used. Metal containers and portable tanks meeting the requirements of and containing products authorized by chapter I, title 49 of the Code of Federal Regulations (regulations issued by the Hazardous Materials Regulations Board, Department of Transportation), shall be deemed to be acceptable.

29 CFR 1910.106(d)(3)(i): Maximum capacity. Not more than 60 gallons of Category 1, 2, or 3 flammable liquids, nor more than 120 gallons of Category 4 flammable liquids, may be stored in a storage cabinet.

29 CFR 1910.106(d)(3)(ii)(a): Metal cabinets constructed in the following manner shall be deemed to be in compliance. The bottom, top, door, and sides of the cabinet shall be at least No. 18-gauge sheet iron and double-walled with 1½-inch air space. Joints shall be riveted, welded, or made tight by some equally effective means. The door shall be provided with a three-point lock, and the door sill shall be raised at least 2 inches above the bottom of the cabinet.

29 CFR 1910.106(d)(4)(i): Construction. Inside storage rooms shall be constructed to meet the required fire-resistive rating for their use. Such construction shall comply with the test specifications set forth in Standard Methods of Fire Tests of Building Construction and Materials, NFPA 251-1969. Where an automatic sprinkler system is provided, the system shall be designed and installed in an acceptable manner. Openings to other rooms or buildings shall be provided with noncombustible liquid-tight raised sills or ramps at least 4 inches in height, or the floor in the storage area shall be at least 4 inches below the surrounding floor. Openings shall be provided with approved self-closing fire doors. The room shall be liquid-tight at the wall-floor junctions. A permissible alternative to the sill or ramp is an open-grated trench inside the room, which drains to a safe location. Where other portions of the building or other properties are exposed, windows shall be protected as set forth in the Standard for Fire Doors and Windows, NFPA No. 80-1968, which is incorporated by reference as specified in § 1910.6, for Class E or F openings. Wood at least 1 inch nominal thickness may be used for shelving, racks, dunnage, scuffboards, floor overlay, and similar installations.	

29 CFR 1910.106(d)(4)(iv): Ventilation. Every inside storage room shall be provided with either a gravity or a mechanical exhaust ventilation system. Such a system shall be designed to provide for a complete change of air within the room at least six times per hour. If a mechanical exhaust system is used, it shall be controlled by a switch located outside the door. The ventilating equipment and any lighting fixtures shall be operated by the same switch. A pilot light shall be installed adjacent to the switch if Category 1 or 2 flammable liquids, or Category 3 flammable liquids with a flashpoint below 100 °F (37.8 °C), are dispensed within the room. Where gravity ventilation is provided, the fresh air intake, as well as the exhaust outlet from the room, shall be on the exterior of the building in which the room is located.

questions.text: Has the facility conducted an assessment of potential hazards resulting from PIT and pedestrian interactions?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: Implement engineering controls, such as physical barriers (guardrails/bollards) separating walkways from vehicle lanes, designated pedestrian crosswalks, convex mirrors at blind intersections, or sensor-based collision avoidance systems reduce struck-by hazards and traffic congestion by making changes to facility layout, aisle design, and traffic flow patterns provide employees with clearly marked designated paths and high-visibility vests and train employees on how to prevent and recognize situational hazards involving mobile equipment, how to apply pedestrian right-of-way rules, and appropriate engineering controls and work practices to reduce the risk of Powered Industrial Truck (PIT) hazards.	

Note: Mere implementation of a change (e.g., painting a line on the floor) is not sufficient to satisfy an abatement requirement; the change must actually reduce the hazard. Documenting "after" assessments or traffic-flow observations provides evidence that the engineering or administrative controls implemented were effective in eliminating or reducing the recognized hazard to an acceptable level.

Note: While OSHA requires that adequate first aid supplies be readily available to employees, ANSI/ISEA Z308.1 serves as the "how-to" guide for specific kit contents, inspection, and replenishment. Although this consensus standard contains specific requirements for inspection and replenishment lists, it is not incorporated by reference into 29 CFR 1910 as a mandatory standard.

questions.text: Do you have adequate AEDs present at this facility?

references.osha:
Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: Implement a Medical Emergency Response Plan that includes the placement of Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs) in accessible locations to address the hazard of sudden cardiac arrest ensure AEDs are available within a timeframe that optimizes survival rates (typically 3-5 minutes from collapse to shock) conduct regular maintenance and inspection of units to ensure functionality (checking pads and batteries) and provide training to designated employees on CPR and the proper use of AEDs to reduce the risk of fatality associated with medical emergencies.	

Note: Mere installation of a device (e.g., mounting a cabinet on the wall) is not sufficient to satisfy an abatement requirement; the equipment must be functional and accessible. Documenting regular monthly inspections (verifying battery/pad expiration) and maintaining active training records provides evidence that the administrative controls and emergency equipment implemented are effective in reducing the recognized hazard.

29 CFR 1910.1030(c)(1)(i): Each employer having an employee(s) with occupational exposure as defined by paragraph (b) of this section shall establish a written Exposure Control Plan designed to eliminate or minimize employee exposure.	

29 CFR 1910.1030(d)(1): Universal precautions shall be observed to prevent contact with blood or other potentially infectious materials. Under circumstances in which differentiation between body fluid types is difficult or impossible, all body fluids shall be considered potentially infectious materials.

ANSI/ISEA Z358.1 serves as the "how-to" guide for establishing performance specifications (e.g., flow rate, temperature), though it is not incorporated by reference into the regulation as a mandatory standard.

questions.text: If noise exposures equal or exceed the Action Level (85 dBA), has a continuing, effective Hearing Conservation Program been implemented?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.95(c)(1): The employer shall administer a continuing, effective hearing conservation program, as described in paragraphs (c) through (o) of this section, whenever employee noise exposures equal or exceed an 8-hour time-weighted average sound level (TWA) of 85 decibels measured on the A scale (slow response) or, equivalently, a dose of fifty percent. For purposes of the hearing conservation program, employee noise exposures shall be computed in accordance with appendix A and Table G-16a, and without regard to any attenuation provided by the use of personal protective equipment.

questions.text: Are feasible administrative or engineering controls utilized to reduce sound levels?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.95(b)(1): When employees are subjected to sound exceeding those listed in Table G-16, feasible administrative or engineering controls shall be utilized. If such controls fail to reduce sound levels within the levels of Table G-16, personal protective equipment shall be provided and used to reduce sound levels within the levels of the table.

questions.text: Where controls fail to reduce exposure, is adequate hearing protection provided and mandatory?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.95(b)(1): When employees are subjected to sound exceeding those listed in Table G-16, feasible administrative or engineering controls shall be utilized. If such controls fail to reduce sound levels within the levels of Table G-16, personal protective equipment shall be provided and used to reduce sound levels within the levels of the table.

questions.text: Have potential employee exposures to airborne contaminants (dust, fumes, vapors) been evaluated to determine if they exceed permissible limits?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.134(d)(1)(iii): The employer shall identify and evaluate the respiratory hazard(s) in the workplace; this evaluation shall include a reasonable estimate of employee exposures to respiratory hazard(s) and an identification of the contaminant's chemical state and physical form. Where the employer cannot identify or reasonably estimate the employee exposure, the employer shall consider the atmosphere to be immediately dangerous to life or health (IDLH).

questions.text: Is effective local exhaust or general ventilation maintained in areas where hazardous air contaminants are generated?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.134(a)(1): In the control of those occupational diseases caused by breathing air contaminated with harmful dusts, fogs, fumes, mists, gases, smokes, sprays, or vapors, the primary objective shall be to prevent atmospheric contamination. This shall be accomplished as far as feasible by accepted engineering control measures (for example, enclosure or confinement of the operation, general and local ventilation, and substitution of less toxic materials).

questions.text: If engineering controls fail to reduce exposure limits, has a written Respiratory Protection Program been implemented in accordance with 29 CFR 1910.134?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.134(d)(1)(iii): The employer shall identify and evaluate the respiratory hazard(s) in the workplace this evaluation shall include a reasonable estimate of employee exposures to respiratory hazard(s) and an identification of the contaminant's chemical state and physical form.

questions.text: Have indoor air quality assessments for mold been conducted in areas with known moisture intrusion, water damage, or high humidity?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.141(a)(3)(i): All places of employment shall be kept clean to the extent that the nature of the work allows.	

Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: Implement engineering controls, such as maintaining building envelopes to prevent water intrusion, ensuring HVAC systems are regularly inspected to keep relative humidity below 60%, and utilizing dehumidifiers or local exhaust ventilation in high-moisture areas reduce biological hazards by identifying and eliminating the source of moisture within 24-48 hours of a water event and removing water-damaged porous materials that cannot be effectively cleaned provide employees involved in remediation with appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) (e.g., N95 respirators, gloves, and eye protection) as determined by a hazard assessment and train employees on how to recognize and report water intrusion or mold growth, the potential health effects of exposure, and the appropriate work practices to maintain a dry working environment.

questions.text: Has an exposure determination been conducted for other regulated hazards present in the facility (e.g., Lead, Asbestos, Silica, Hexavalent Chromium) as required by their specific OSHA standards?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.1026(d)(1) Hexavalent Chromium: General. Each employer who has a workplace or work operation covered by this section shall determine the 8-hour TWA exposure for each employee exposed to chromium (VI).	

29 CFR 1910.1025(d)(2) Lead: Initial determination. Each employer who has a workplace or work operation covered by this standard shall determine if any employee may be exposed to lead at or above the action level.	

29 CFR 1910.1025(d)(3)(i): Basis of initial determination. (A) The employer shall monitor employee exposures and shall base initial determinations on the employee exposure monitoring results and any of the following, relevant considerations: (1) Any information, observations, or calculations which would indicate employee exposure to lead (2) Any previous measurements of airborne lead and (3) Any employee complaints of symptoms which may be attributable to exposure to lead.	

29 CFR 1910.1001(d)(2)(i): Asbestos: Initial monitoring. Each employer who has a workplace or work operation covered by this standard shall perform monitoring to determine accurately the airborne concentrations of asbestos to which employees may be exposed	

29 CFR 1910.1053(d)(1): Silica: The employer shall assess the exposure of each employee who is or may reasonably be expected to be exposed to respirable crystalline silica at or above the action level in accordance with either the performance option in paragraph (d)(2) or the scheduled monitoring option in paragraph (d)(3) of this section.	

29 CFR 1910.1053(d)(2): Silica: Performance option. The employer shall assess the 8-hour TWA exposure for each employee on the basis of any combination of air monitoring data or objective data sufficient to accurately characterize employee exposures to respirable crystalline silica.	

29 CFR 1910.1053(d)(3)(i): Silica: Scheduled monitoring option. The employer shall perform initial monitoring to assess the 8-hour TWA exposure for each employee on the basis of one or more personal breathing zone air samples that reflect the exposures of employees on each shift, for each job classification, in each work area.

questions.text: Are employees using the required PPE for this facility?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.132(a): Protective equipment, including personal protective equipment for eyes, face, head, and extremities, protective clothing, respiratory devices, and protective shields and barriers, shall be provided, used, and maintained in a sanitary and reliable condition wherever it is necessary by reason of hazards of processes or environment, chemical hazards, radiological hazards, or mechanical irritants encountered in a manner capable of causing injury or impairment in the function of any part of the body through absorption, inhalation or physical contact.

29 CFR 1910.132(d)(1): The employer shall assess the workplace to determine if hazards are present, or are likely to be present, which necessitate the use of personal protective equipment (PPE). If such hazards are present, the employer must select the types of PPE that will protect the affected employee, communicate selection decisions to each affected employee, and select PPE that properly fits each affected employee.

questions.text: Do you have a current workplace hazard assessment to determine the required PPE for each job in this facility?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.132(d)(1): The employer shall assess the workplace to determine if hazards are present, or are likely to be present, which necessitate the use of personal protective equipment (PPE). If such hazards are present, the employer must select the types of PPE that will protect the affected employee, communicate selection decisions to each affected employee, and select PPE that properly fits each affected employee.

29 CFR 1910.132(d)(2): The employer shall verify that the required workplace hazard assessment has been performed through a written certification that identifies the workplace evaluated; the person certifying that the evaluation has been performed; the date(s) of the hazard assessment; and, which identifies the document as a certification of hazard assessment.

questions.text: Do employees receive training on the selection and use of PPE?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.132(f)(1): The employer shall provide training to each employee who is required by this section to use PPE. Each such employee shall be trained to know at least the following: (i) When PPE is necessary; (ii) What PPE is necessary; (iii) How to properly don, doff, adjust, and wear PPE; (iv) The limitations of the PPE; and, (v) The proper care, maintenance, useful life and disposal of the PPE.

questions.text: Is PPE of safe design or construction for the work to be performed?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.132(c): Design. All personal protective equipment shall be of safe design and construction for the work to be performed.

questions.text: Is the required PPE properly fitted?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.132(d)(1)(iii): Select PPE that properly fits each affected employee.

questions.text: Are simultaneously worn PPE items compatible and effective together?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.134(g)(1)(i)(B): States: "Do not interfere with the seal of the facepiece." (Specific to respirators, not general PPE).

questions.text: Is all PPE maintained in a sanitary condition and ready for use?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.132(a): Protective equipment, including personal protective equipment for eyes, face, head, and extremities, protective clothing, respiratory devices, and protective shields and barriers, shall be provided, used, and maintained in a sanitary and reliable condition wherever it is necessary by reason of hazards of processes or environment, chemical hazards, radiological hazards, or mechanical irritants encountered in a manner capable of causing injury or impairment in the function of any part of the body through absorption, inhalation or physical contact.

questions.text: Have you conducted a workplace hazard assessment to determine which jobs and areas in this facility require protective footwear?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.132(d)(1): The employer shall assess the workplace to determine if hazards are present, or are likely to be present, which necessitate the use of personal protective equipment (PPE). If such hazards are present, the employer must select the types of PPE that will protect the affected employee, communicate selection decisions to each affected employee, and select PPE that properly fits each affected employee.

29 CFR 1910.136(a): The employer shall ensure that each affected employee uses protective footwear when working in areas where there is a danger of foot injuries due to falling or rolling objects, or objects piercing the sole, or when the use of protective footwear will protect the affected employee from an electrical hazard, such as a static-discharge or electric-shock hazard, that remains after the employer takes other necessary protective measures.

questions.text: Are employees wearing protective safety footwear in areas where applicable hazards exist? (e.g., impact, compression, electrical, static, and puncture hazards)

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.136(a): General requirements. The employer shall ensure that each affected employee uses protective footwear when working in areas where there is a danger of foot injuries due to falling or rolling objects, or objects piercing the sole, or when the use of protective footwear will protect the affected employee from an electrical hazard, such as a static-discharge or electric-shock hazard, that remains after the employer takes other necessary protective measures.

questions.text: Do you provide protective footwear to each affected employee through a subsidy, stipend, or other footwear program?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.132(h)(1): Except as provided by paragraphs (h)(2) through (h)(6) of this section, the protective equipment, including personal protective equipment (PPE), used to comply with this part, shall be provided by the employer at no cost to employees.	

29 CFR 1910.132(h)(2): The employer is not required to pay for non-specialty safety-toe protective footwear (including steel-toe shoes or steel-toe boots) and non-specialty prescription safety eyewear, provided that the employer permits such items to be worn off the job-site.

title: Safety and Health Programs and Recordkeeping

questions.text: Does your written safety and health program address all of the following: management commitment, employee involvement, worksite analyses, hazard prevention and control, and training?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.1200(e)(1): HazCom: Employers shall develop, implement, and maintain at each workplace, a written hazard communication program which at least describes how the criteria specified in paragraphs (f), (g), and (h) of this section for labels and other forms of warning, safety data sheets, and employee information and training will be met, and which also includes the following: A list of the hazardous chemicals known to be present using a product identifier that is referenced on the appropriate safety data sheet (the list may be compiled for the workplace as a whole or for individual work areas) and, The methods the employer will use to inform employees of the hazards of non-routine tasks (for example, the cleaning of reactor vessels), and the hazards associated with chemicals contained in unlabeled pipes in their work areas.	

29 CFR 1910.134(c)(1): Respiratory Protection: In any workplace where respirators are necessary to protect the health of the employee or whenever respirators are required by the employer, the employer shall establish and implement a written respiratory protection program with worksite-specific procedures.	

29 CFR 1910.1030(c)(1)(i): Bloodborne Pathogens: Each employer having an employee(s) with occupational exposure as defined by paragraph (b) of this section shall establish a written Exposure Control Plan designed to eliminate or minimize employee exposure	

29 CFR 1910.38(b): Emergency Action Plans: Written and oral emergency action plans. An emergency action plan must be in writing, kept in the workplace, and available to employees for review. However, an employer with 10 or fewer employees may communicate the plan orally to employees.	

29 CFR 1910.39(b): Fire Prevention Plans: Written and oral fire prevention plans. A fire prevention plan must be in writing, be kept in the workplace, and be made available to employees for review. However, an employer with 10 or fewer employees may communicate the plan orally to employees.	

29 CFR 1910.146(c)(4): Permit-Required Confined Spaces: If the employer decides that its employees will enter permit spaces, the employer shall develop and implement a written permit space program that complies with this section.	

29 CFR 1910.147(c)(4)(i): Lockout/Tagout: Procedures. Procedures shall be developed, documented, and utilized for the control of potentially hazardous energy when employees are engaged in the activities covered by this section.	

29 CFR 1910.119(f)(1): Process Safety Management: The employer shall develop and implement written operating procedures that provide clear instructions for safely conducting activities involved in each covered process consistent with the process safety information and shall address at least the following elements.

questions.text: Does your recordkeeping process ensure all of the following: Obtaining a report on every injury or job-related illness requiring medical treatment Recording each injury or job-related illness on the OSHA Form 300 Preparing a supplementary record for recordable cases on an OSHA Form 301 or other equivalent forms Preparing an annual summary using OSHA Form 300 Posting the OSHA Form 300A from February 1 - May 1 If required, electronically submitting the Form 300A to OSHA Retaining these records for at least five years?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1904.7(a): Basic requirement. You must consider an injury or illness to be recordable, and therefore enter it on the OSHA 300 Log, if it results in any of the following: death, days away from work, restricted work or transfer to another job, medical treatment beyond first aid, or loss of consciousness.	
29 CFR 1904.29(a): Basic requirement. You must use OSHA 300, 300-A, and 301 forms, or equivalent forms, for recordable injuries and illnesses. The OSHA 300 form is called the Log of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses, the OSHA 300-A is the Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses, and the OSHA 301 form is the Injury and Illness Incident Report.	

29 CFR 1904.32(b)(6): When do I have to post the annual summary? You must post the annual summary no later than February 1 of the year following the year covered by the records and keep the posting in place until April 30.	

29 CFR 1904.33(a): Basic requirement. You must save the OSHA 300 Log, the privacy case list (if one exists), the annual summary, and the OSHA 301 Incident Report forms for five (5) years following the end of the calendar year that these records cover.	

29 CFR 1904.41(a)(1)(i): If your establishment had 20–249 employees at any time during the previous calendar year, and your establishment is classified in an industry listed in appendix A to subpart E of this part, then you must electronically submit information from OSHA Form 300A Summary of WorkRelated Injuries and Illnesses to OSHA or OSHA’s designee. You must submit the information once a year, no later than the date listed in paragraph (c) of this section of the year after the calendar year covered by the form.	

29 CFR 1904.41(a)(1)(ii): If your establishment had 250 or more employees at any time during the previous calendar year, and this part requires your establishment to keep records, then you must electronically submit information from OSHA Form 300A Summary of Work-Related Injuries and Illnesses to OSHA or OSHA’s designee. You must submit the information once a year, no later than the date listed in paragraph (c) of this section of the year after the calendar year covered by the form.

questions.text: If required, are records on the exposure of employees to toxic substances and hazardous exposures, physical examination reports, and employment records maintained?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.1020(d)(1): Employee Medical Records: Unless a specific occupational safety and health standard provides a different period of time, each employer shall assure the preservation and retention of records as follows: The medical record for each employee shall be preserved and maintained for at least the duration of employment plus thirty (30) years, except that the following types of records need not be retained for any specified period: (A) Health insurance claims records maintained separately from the employer's medical program and its records, (B) First aid records (not including medical histories) of one-time treatment and subsequent observation of minor scratches, cuts, burns, splinters, and the like which do not involve medical treatment, loss of consciousness, restriction of work or motion, or transfer to another job, if made on-site by a non-physician and if maintained separately from the employer's medical program and its records, and (C) The medical records of employees who have worked for less than (1) year for the employer need not be retained beyond the term of employment if they are provided to the employee upon the termination of employment.	

29 CFR 1910.1020(d)(1)(ii): Employee Exposure Records: Each employee exposure record shall be preserved and maintained for at least thirty (30) years, except that: (A) Background data to environmental (workplace) monitoring or measuring, such as laboratory reports and worksheets, need only be retained for one (1) year as long as the sampling results, the collection methodology (sampling plan), a description of the analytical and mathematical methods used, and a summary of other background data relevant to interpretation of the results obtained, are retained for at least thirty (30) years and (B) Safety data sheets and paragraph (c)(5)(iv) records concerning the identity of a substance or agent need not be retained for any specified period as long as some record of the identity (chemical name if known) of the substance or agent, where it was used, and when it was used is retained for at least thirty (30) years and (C) Biological monitoring results designated as exposure records by specific occupational safety and health standards shall be preserved and maintained as required by the specific standard.

questions.text: Are all hazards or dangerous conditions adequately communicated using signs, lights, alarms, or other methods at this facility?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.145(a)(1): These specifications apply to the design, application, and use of signs or symbols (as included in paragraphs (c) through (e) of this section) intended to indicate and, insofar as possible, to define specific hazards of a nature such that failure to designate them may lead to accidental injury to workers or the public, or both, or to property damage.	

29 CFR 1910.145(f)(3): Use. Tags shall be used as a means to prevent accidental injury or illness to employees who are exposed to hazardous or potentially hazardous conditions, equipment or operations which are out of the ordinary, unexpected or not readily apparent. Tags shall be used until such time as the identified hazard is eliminated or the hazardous operation is completed.	

29 CFR 1910.165(b)(1): The employee alarm system shall provide warning for necessary emergency action as called for in the emergency action plan, or for reaction time for safe escape of employees from the workplace or the immediate work area, or both.	

29 CFR 1910.144(a)(1): Red. Red shall be the basic color for the identification of: Fire protection equipment and apparatus.	

29 CFR 1910.144(a)(3): Yellow. Yellow shall be the basic color for designating caution and for marking physical hazards such as: Striking against, stumbling, falling, tripping, and "caught in between."

questions.text: Are there written security policies for your facility?

references.osha: Section 5(a)(1) of the Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970: Each employer shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees. Employers should, therefore: Implement engineering controls, such as closed-circuit television (CCTV) cameras to monitor high-risk areas, physical access control systems (keycards/badge readers) at employee entrances, and adequate exterior lighting in parking lots and walkways; reduce security risks by developing and implementing a written workplace security policy that outlines "Zero Tolerance" for violence and procedures for unauthorized visitors; provide employees with clear communication channels for reporting security concerns without fear of reprisal; and train employees on the specific facility security policies, how to recognize behavioral indicators of potential violence, and the emergency response procedures for security breaches or active threats.

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.37(b)(1): Each exit route must be adequately lighted so that an employee with normal vision can see along the exit route.	

29 CFR 1910.305(a)(2)(ix): All lamps for general illumination shall be protected from accidental contact or breakage by a suitable fixture or lampholder with a guard. Brass shell, paper-lined sockets, or other metal-cased sockets may not be used unless the shell is grounded.

questions.text: Do you have practices, products, or procedures in place to minimize slips, trips, and falls?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.22(a)(1): All places of employment, passageways, storerooms, service rooms, and walking-working surfaces are kept in a clean, orderly, and sanitary condition.	

29 CFR 1910.22(a)(2): The floor of each workroom is maintained in a clean and, so far as possible, a dry condition. When wet processes are used, drainage must be maintained and, to the extent feasible, dry standing places, such as false floors, platforms, and mats must be provided.	

29 CFR 1910.22(a)(3): Walking-working surfaces are maintained free of hazards such as sharp or protruding objects, loose boards, corrosion, leaks, spills, snow, and ice.

questions.text: When working at elevated heights, is fall protection equipment being used?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.28(b)(1)(i): Unprotected sides and edges. (i) Except as provided elsewhere in this section, the employer must ensure that each employee on a walking-working surface with an unprotected side or edge that is 4 feet (1.2 m) or more above a lower level is protected from falling by one or more of the following: (A) Guardrail systems (B) Safety net systems or (C) Personal fall protection systems, such as personal fall arrest, travel restraint, or positioning systems.

questions.text: Are all employees who use personal fall protection systems... trained on fall and equipment hazards?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.30(a)(1): Fall hazards. Before any employee is exposed to a fall hazard, the employer must provide training for each employee who uses personal fall protection systems or who is required to be trained as specified elsewhere in this subpart. Employers must ensure employees are trained in the requirements of this paragraph on or before May 17, 2017.	

29 CFR 1910.30(a)(2): The employer must ensure that each employee is trained by a qualified person.

questions.text: Do you have a process for inspecting portable ladders?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.23(b)(9): Ladders are inspected before initial use in each work shift, and more frequently as necessary, to identify any visible defects that could cause employee injury.

questions.text: Do your fixed ladders meet the requirements of the latest walking working surfaces standard (i.e., self-closing safety gates, ladder safety systems as required)?

references.osha:
29 CFR 1910.29(b)(13): When guardrail systems are used around holes which are used as points of access (such as ladderways), they shall be provided with a self-closing gate that slides or swings away from the hole, and is equipped with a top rail and midrail or equivalent intermediate member that meets the requirements in paragraph (b) of this section, or is offset so that a person cannot walk directly into the hole.	

29 CFR 1910.28(b)(9)(i): For fixed ladders that extend more than 24 feet (7.3 m) above a lower level, the employer must ensure: (A) Existing fixed ladders. Each fixed ladder installed before November 19, 2018 is equipped with a personal fall arrest system, ladder safety system, cage, or well (B) New fixed ladders. Each fixed ladder installed on and after November 19, 2018, is equipped with a personal fall arrest system or a ladder safety system (C) Replacement. When a fixed ladder, cage, or well, or any portion of a section thereof, is replaced, a personal fall arrest system or ladder safety system is installed in at least that section of the fixed ladder, cage, or well where the replacement is located and (D) Final deadline. On and after November 18, 2036, all fixed ladders are equipped with a personal fall arrest system or a ladder safety system.

questions.text: Are all walking and working surfaces inspected regularly and maintained in a safe condition?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.22(d)(1): Inspection, maintenance, and repair. The employer must ensure: (i) Walking-working surfaces are inspected, regularly and as necessary, and maintained in a safe condition (ii) Hazardous conditions on walking-working surfaces are corrected or repaired before an employee uses the walking-working surface again.

questions.text: Are employees adequately protected from exposure to falling objects?

references.osha: 29 CFR 1910.28(c)(1): Protection from falling objects. When an employee is exposed to falling objects, the employer must ensure that each employee wears head protection that meets the requirements of subpart I of this part. In addition, the employer must protect employees from falling objects by implementing one or more of the following: (1) Erecting toeboards, screens, or guardrail systems to prevent objects from falling to a lower level (2) Erecting canopy structures and keeping potential falling objects far enough from an edge, hole, or opening to prevent them from falling to a lower level or (3) Barricading the area into which objects could fall, prohibiting employees from entering the barricaded area, and keeping objects far enough from an edge or opening to prevent them from falling to a lower level.
